metadata.title: AI IDE feature matrix 2026
metadata.purpose: Machine-readable comparison of modern AI IDE features and Vox implementation opportunities.

methodology.primary_sources: Official docs, official release notes, official changelogs, and official product pages were preferred. Community or secondary sources are only reflected indirectly through lowered confidence notes.
methodology.scoring_note: Composite scores are synthesized from documented feature coverage, workflow safety, extensibility, and review ergonomics. They are not benchmark scores.

products.name: Cursor
products.pricing_signal: Usage-based premium IDE with free and paid tiers.
products.notes: Best-documented all-around agent IDE in this comparison set; unusually strong mode separation, subagents, and review affordances.

products.name: Windsurf
products.pricing_signal: Premium AI IDE with strong workspace customizations and enterprise policy surface.
products.notes: Especially strong on memories, rules, reusable workflows, and real-time awareness.

products.name: Antigravity
products.pricing_signal: Public preview positioning, pricing surface not mature in fetched evidence.
products.notes: Compelling directionally, but evidence quality is weaker than the rest of the set and should be treated cautiously.

products.name: Claude Code
products.pricing_signal: Model-usage-driven agent workflow rather than conventional IDE subscription.
products.notes: Not an IDE in the classic sense, but one of the strongest references for tool-use architecture, hooks, and subagents.

products.name: ChatGPT desktop plus Codex app
products.category: Desktop AI app plus coding workspace
products.pricing_signal: Separate ChatGPT app tiers plus Codex workflow value in desktop app and coding surfaces.
products.notes: Strong on worktrees, integrated terminal, voice, IDE sync, and Git review controls.

products.name: Gemini Code Assist
products.pricing_signal: Free, Standard, and Enterprise tiers with IDE and enterprise positioning.
products.notes: Strong breadth across chat, context targeting, outlines, finish-changes, and rules, but not as open-ended as Cursor or Claude Code.

products.name: GitHub Copilot coding agent
products.pricing_signal: Integrated into Copilot plan tiers and broader GitHub workflow value.
products.notes: Especially strong when the desired workflow is issue-to-PR and GitHub-native collaboration.

products.name: Zed AI
products.pricing_signal: Editor-first with hosted and BYO-provider options.
products.notes: Excellent example of how to keep agent features powerful while still feeling editor-native and review-friendly.

products.name: Aider
products.pricing_signal: Open-source CLI with model costs depending on the provider you choose.
products.notes: A strong reference for Git-centric safety and fast local coding workflows, but not for GUI-heavy IDE ergonomics.

products.name: Cline
products.pricing_signal: Extension-first agent workflow with provider-dependent cost.
products.notes: A good reference for explicit plan-versus-act ergonomics and checkpoint behavior.

products.name: Roo Code
products.pricing_signal: Tool-and-provider-driven rather than a single closed paid IDE surface.
products.notes: Strong reference for mode design and orchestration delegation in constrained contexts.

products.name: Replit Agent
products.pricing_signal: Core and Pro plans with explicit Agent billing and mode differentiation.
products.notes: Cloud-first and more end-user friendly than most IDE tools; strong on checkpoints, app testing, and visual canvas workflows.

products.name: Devin
products.pricing_signal: Premium autonomous agent workflow positioned above ordinary assistant pricing.
products.notes: A strong reference for planning, codebase indexing, persistent knowledge, and long-running autonomous sessions.

products.name: Continue
products.pricing_signal: Open configuration surface; value comes from configurability, models, and community hub rather than a single polished premium UX.
products.notes: Best thought of as a highly configurable AI plumbing layer rather than a polished opinionated AI IDE.

vox_baseline.summary: Vox already has unusually deep backend infrastructure for planning, tasks, snapshots, browser tooling, memory, and retrieval. The primary gap is not raw capability count but developer-facing productization, transparency, and review ergonomics inside the extension.

feature_matrix.name: Inline edits and low-latency completion
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: This is the highest-frequency AI loop in daily coding and often the first feature developers fall in love with.

vox.implementation_locus: Mostly extension polish and MCP registry parity, with optional Mens latency work later.
vox.notes: Close an obvious UX gap by tightening latency, discoverability, acceptance controls, and MCP parity around ghost text.

feature_matrix.name: Agentic multi-file execution
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: This is the major time-saver beyond autocomplete: natural-language tasks turn into coordinated multi-file work.

vox.implementation_locus: Extension UX plus task-orchestrator handoff and richer apply/review loops.
vox.notes: The backend already exists; what is missing is a best-in-class agent workspace that makes task execution obvious, inspectable, and pleasant.

feature_matrix.name: Mode separation: ask, plan, debug, execute
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Developers trust AI more when there is a crisp boundary between exploration, planning, and action.

vox.implementation_locus: Extension command surface and webview state model.
vox.notes: Vox already has plan tooling. Turning it into explicit first-class user modes would significantly improve trust and user education.

feature_matrix.name: Checkpoints, revert, and review UX
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Safety is one of the biggest drivers of developer affection for agentic tools because it lowers the emotional cost of experimentation.

vox.implementation_locus: Mostly extension/UI work on top of existing snapshot and workspace machinery.
vox.notes: This is one of the clearest 'already have the backend, need the product surface' opportunities in Vox.

feature_matrix.name: Terminal, browser, and tool-use transparency
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Advanced developers want autonomy, but only when they can see what the agent is doing and why.

vox.implementation_locus: Extension/webview event model and chat transcript rendering, not core capability invention.
vox.notes: Vox exposes many tools already. The missing piece is a chat thread or dashboard that narrates and reviews those tool calls coherently.

feature_matrix.name: Subagents, parallelism, and orchestration
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: This is a major unlock for larger tasks and one of the clearest separators between basic assistants and serious AI IDEs.

vox.implementation_locus: Substantial extension and orchestration UX work, but with strong backend foundations already present.
vox.notes: Vox can differentiate here long term, but should first improve single-agent transparency and review workflows.

feature_matrix.name: Context targeting, indexing, search, and mentions
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Good context controls reduce hallucinations and make AI feel fast rather than clumsy.

vox.implementation_locus: Blend extension affordances with search-ranking polish and context envelope rollout.
vox.notes: This is a prime area for Mens later, but even before model training there is large product value in better context picking and retrieval defaults.

feature_matrix.name: Rules, memories, workflows, and skills
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Persistent customization is where casual usefulness becomes repeatable team productivity.

vox.implementation_locus: Extension-facing UX for repository rules and reusable workflows, backed by existing memory and skills surfaces.
vox.notes: A repo-visible rules/workflows lane would be one of the highest leverage ways to make Vox feel sticky without changing the language.

feature_matrix.name: Extensibility: MCP, hooks, custom agents, custom tools
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Advanced users and teams want their IDE to plug into existing systems, not replace them.

vox.implementation_locus: Mostly discoverability, docs, and UI surfacing rather than backend build-out.
vox.notes: This is already a Vox strength. The work is to surface it visibly and make it pleasant to use from the extension.

feature_matrix.name: Git, PR, and workspace isolation
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: The more autonomous the agent, the more developers demand safe isolation and credible review paths.

vox.implementation_locus: Deep integration work if Vox wants parity with Git-native worktrees and PR loops.
vox.notes: Useful, but not the first thing to build unless Vox wants to compete directly with Codex or GitHub-native agent workflows.

feature_matrix.name: Multimodal input and GUI surfaces
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: Voice, images, canvases, and visual review make AI feel more like a product than just a chat box.

vox.implementation_locus: Primarily extension/webview work; Mens and Oratio become differentiators later rather than prerequisites now.
vox.notes: Vox already has more here than most repos would; the next step is coherence and polish rather than raw feature count.

feature_matrix.name: Automated verification, diagnostics, and autofix loops
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: The best developer feeling is not just fast generation but fast confident closure after tests, lints, and evidence checks pass.

vox.implementation_locus: UI-driven verification flows on top of already-shipped test, check, and adequacy tooling.
vox.notes: This is one of the easiest high-value wins because most of the core logic already exists in MCP and orchestrator layers.

feature_matrix.name: Collaboration, tracking, and shareability
feature_matrix.why_it_matters: These features matter most after core single-user loops already feel excellent.

vox.implementation_locus: Would require product design decisions, not just engineering effort.
vox.notes: Important later, but should come after trust, context, review, and rules/workflows.

priority_recommendations.rationale: Highest user-trust gain with strong existing Vox backend leverage.
Turns one-off usefulness into repeatable team productivity without changing the Vox language.
Improves quality, speed, and differentiation while also preparing the ground for Mens.
Low-to-medium effort UX improvement with high trust payoff.
Pairs well with existing Vox checks and reduces the gap between agent output and developer confidence.

defer_or_limit.reason: Useful but expensive and less urgent than review, context, and workflow customization.
Vox should productize single-agent trust and transparency before going deeper on visible multi-agent complexity.

mens_specific_opportunities: Use Mens for low-latency completion and edit routing once the extension UX is already compelling.
Use Mens or search-ranking models to improve context selection and retrieval quality rather than leading with a model-first strategy.
Use Mens to personalize rules, workflow suggestions, and voice-to-code only after deterministic repository-visible controls exist.
